executiveSummary.summary: Strong problem-market fit with rural telehealth gap, experienced team, and non-dilutive funding. Key risks include regulatory complexity, slow clinic adoption cycles, and specialist recruitment. The B2B2C model through rural clinics is smart distribution strategy. Success depends on execution of first state launch and proving unit economics.

marketSize.tam: $29.1B US telehealth market (McKinsey 2023), with rural representing ~19% of population
marketSize.sam: $5.5B rural telehealth opportunity based on rural population and healthcare spending patterns
marketSize.som: $50-100M in 3-5 target states with 500-1000 rural clinics, assuming 10-20% penetration
marketSize.methodology: Based on rural population (60M), average healthcare spend per capita ($4,500), and telehealth adoption rates in rural areas (currently 15-25%)

competitors.name: Teladoc
competitors.weakness: Not optimized for rural connectivity/demographics, expensive for small clinics

competitors.name: Doxy.me
competitors.description: Simple telehealth platform popular with smaller practices
competitors.weakness: Limited rural-specific features, basic EHR integration

competitors.name: SimplePractice
competitors.description: Practice management with telehealth for small practices
competitors.strength: Integrated practice management, good for solo providers
competitors.weakness: Not specialized for rural challenges, limited specialist network

competitors.name: MDLive
competitors.weakness: Urban-centric, doesn't integrate with rural clinic workflows

marketAnalysis.positioning: The only telehealth platform built specifically for rural healthcare delivery - combining low-bandwidth optimization, simplified UX for older patients, and deep rural clinic integration
marketAnalysis.timing: Perfect timing with permanent telehealth reimbursement changes post-COVID, rural health workforce shortages, and increased federal funding for rural health initiatives
gaps: No major player focused specifically on rural telehealth
Existing platforms require high bandwidth and tech-savvy users
Poor integration with rural clinic workflows and EHR systems

valueProposition.coreProblem: Rural patients can't access specialists due to geographic barriers, and existing telehealth platforms don't work well with rural infrastructure and patient demographics
valueProposition.targetCustomer: Rural health networks and independent rural clinics (25-200 beds) serving populations under 50,000, particularly those with older patient demographics and limited IT resources
valueProposition.uniqueAngle: Only platform optimized for rural constraints: low-bandwidth connectivity, older/less tech-savvy patients, small clinic operations, and complex rural insurance mix
valueProposition.whyNow: COVID accelerated telehealth adoption, CMS expanded rural telehealth reimbursement permanently, and rural physician shortages have reached crisis levels

businessModel.revenueStrategy: Dual revenue: $2,000/month SaaS fee per clinic plus $15 transaction fee per specialist visit, targeting 60% SaaS / 40% transaction mix
businessModel.pricingFramework: $2,000/month justified by replacing need for locum specialists ($2,000-5,000/day), $15/visit competitive with specialist referral coordination costs

unitEconomics.estimatedCAC: $15,000-25,000 per clinic (18-month sales cycle, high-touch onboarding)

businessModel.scalabilityAssessment: At 10x scale (500 clinics), need robust specialist network, automated onboarding, and multi-state licensing compliance. Revenue potential $150M+ annually.

technicalFeasibility.recommendedStack: WebRTC for video (with bandwidth adaptation), React/Node.js, AWS (HIPAA-compliant), Twilio for communications, Epic/Cerner integration APIs

buildVsBuy.rationale: Agora.io or Twilio Video handle bandwidth optimization and HIPAA compliance
Rural EHR integration is highly custom, competitive advantage
Use Stripe or similar, focus on core platform

technicalRisks: EHR integration complexity varies significantly by vendor
Low-bandwidth video quality may still be insufficient in some areas
technicalFeasibility.estimatedMvpTimeline: 6-9 months for basic MVP, 12-15 months for full featured platform

risks.description: Multi-state medical licensing, HIPAA compliance, and varying telehealth regulations could create significant barriers and costs
risks.mitigation: Start in one telehealth-friendly state, hire healthcare compliance counsel, get SOC 2 certification early

risks.description: Rural clinic technology adoption is notoriously slow (18-24 month sales cycles), could burn through funding before achieving scale
risks.mitigation: Start with existing clinic relationships, offer pilot programs, demonstrate clear ROI with case studies

risks.description: Recruiting specialists willing to serve rural patients at competitive rates while maintaining quality
risks.mitigation: Partner with academic medical centers, offer competitive rates, start with high-demand specialties like mental health

risks.description: Telehealth reimbursement rates and policies could change, affecting clinic willingness to pay
risks.mitigation: Diversify revenue beyond reimbursement-dependent visits, build strong ROI case beyond reimbursement

risks.description: Rural internet infrastructure may be too poor even for optimized platform in some areas
risks.mitigation: Partner with rural broadband initiatives, develop offline-capable features, focus on areas with minimum viable connectivity

week1to4: Choose first state (recommend Texas - large rural population, telehealth-friendly)
Interview 15 rural clinic administrators about current pain points and technology adoption
Map out Texas medical licensing requirements and timeline
week5to8: Develop detailed MVP specification based on clinic interviews
Begin recruiting 2-3 pilot clinics in Texas
Start specialist recruitment for mental health and dermatology
Create regulatory compliance roadmap with legal counsel
week9to12: Develop specialist onboarding and credentialing process

decisionAgenda.question: Which state should we launch in first?

options.tradeoff: Large market, telehealth-friendly regulations vs competitive landscape
Less competition, strong rural need vs smaller market, complex geography
Good rural health infrastructure vs more restrictive telehealth laws

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Texas - largest rural population (3.8M), favorable telehealth regulations, strong health network infrastructure, and your Epic background gives credibility with larger health systems

decisionAgenda.question: Which specialty should we start with?

options.tradeoff: High demand, good reimbursement, easier licensing vs crowded telehealth market
Visual specialty ideal for telehealth, clear ROI vs limited emergency need
Broadest market, clinic familiarity vs complex care coordination needs

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Mental health - highest rural shortage (65% of rural areas lack psychiatrists), strong telehealth reimbursement, and simpler technology requirements for MVP

decisionAgenda.question: Should we pursue additional SBIR Phase II funding ($1.5M) or seek private investment?

options.tradeoff: Non-dilutive, healthcare focus vs 6-month application process, uncertainty
Faster access to capital, industry expertise vs equity dilution, pressure for quick returns

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Apply for SBIR Phase II while simultaneously building relationships with healthcare-focused angels as backup. The non-dilutive nature and healthcare focus of SBIR aligns well with your long development timeline
